name: Facility footprint optimization and expansion planning
consequences: Altering the footprint directly changes enrollment capacity and operating risk. Expansion to twelve suites increases throughput but raises capital costs and staffing requirements; maintaining eight suites keeps costs and risk lower but may limit data yield; reallocating space toward data infrastructure prioritizes measurement quality with potential throughput constraints.
options: Expand to twelve suites with phased staffing and revised safety protocols to support longer stays.
Maintain eight suites and optimize throughput through scheduling and workflow improvements.
Reallocate space toward data infrastructure and equipment while keeping eight suites to maximize data quality per participant.
review: This lever centers on the tension between scale and safety and cost. The options miss a staged, compliance-first expansion pathway that could reduce upfront risk.

name: Sensing modality strategy
consequences: Choosing the sensing approach determines data richness and operating burden. A mixed protocol balances data quality with participant burden and cost, but requires robust data integration. Full PSG on all nights maximizes resolution but increases workload, staffing, and potential participant fatigue.
options: Adopt a mixed-sensor protocol with nightly dry-electrode EEG, contact-free sensors, and episodic PSG on key nights.
Move toward continuous full PSG montages on all nights to maximize data fidelity.
Limit monitoring to low-burden dry-electrode headbands and infrared cameras to maximize throughput.
review: This lever balances data richness versus participant burden and cost, but the options miss a unified data-integration plan that ensures cross-modality harmonization.

name: Recruitment strategy optimization
consequences: Recruitment strategy directly shapes cohort size and representativeness. Broadening recruitment accelerates enrollment and improves external validity but increases coordination and screening burden; hospital-centric recruitment simplifies workflows but may limit participant diversity and generalizability; adaptive enrollment provides responsiveness but risks timeline drift.
options: Broaden recruitment through regional sleep clinics and community partnerships with enhanced participant compensation.
Concentrate recruitment within hospital clinics to streamline consent and ensure high data quality.
Implement adaptive enrollment targets with interim performance reviews and external recruitment consultants.
review: This lever weighs breadth of access against operational simplicity, but the options miss a plan to ensure diversity and data quality across sites.

name: Data governance and privacy posture
consequences: Data governance determines collaboration potential and participant privacy. Tight privacy controls protect participants but slow external collaboration; a data enclave enables controlled access with governance but increases overhead; synthetic data sharing improves openness but reduces granularity.
options: Enforce strict de-identification and access controls with a formal data-use agreement framework.
Establish a controlled data enclave offering tiered access to external researchers under governance.
Publish synthetic data summaries to share patterns while preventing re-identification.
review: This lever balances privacy with collaboration, but the options overlook standardized data-sharing templates across partners. Without an implementation template, external collaborations may stall.

name: Event-triage development strategy
consequences: Semi-automated triage strategies influence review throughput and accuracy. A semi-automated tool with clinician adjudication can raise efficiency but requires robust training data; maintaining dual-human scoring preserves reliability but slows progress; cross-site validation expands generalizability but may delay deployment.
options: Develop and validate a semi-automated triage tool with clinician adjudication on all flagged events.
Maintain dual-human scoring as gold standard while progressively integrating automation.
Pilot cross-site validation with external dataset to test generalizability before deployment.
review: This lever balances automation with clinical oversight, but the options miss a plan for incremental field testing in a single-site pilot to calibrate thresholds.

name: Funding strategy and governance
consequences: Funding strategy governs sustainability and speed of progress. Milestone-based funding aligns resources with progress but risks schedule slippage; pharma collaboration preserves publication rights but may invite perceived conflicts; diversified funding reduces financial risk but can complicate governance.
options: Institute milestone-based funding releases with external review triggers to enforce accountability.
Negotiate enterprise-style collaboration with pharma while preserving publication rights.
Pursue diversified funding from public grants and philanthropic sources to reduce reliance on industry.
review: This lever embeds financial discipline with scholarly freedom, but the options miss an integrated framework for long-term self-sufficiency beyond grant cycles.

name: Overnight staffing model optimization for eight-suites operations
consequences: Pulling this lever reconfigures night-shift coverage around eight active suites, aiming to improve incident response and safety monitoring. It can reduce response delays during parasomnia events but may raise labor costs and staff fatigue if rotations are too aggressive. Downstream, it influences training demands, scheduling complexity, and morale, with potential impacts on retention and coverage reliability.
options: Recalibrate night-shift staffing to align coverage with peak parasomnia events while maintaining safety, training, and burnout mitigation.
Institute a tiered responder model where on-site staff initiate remote escalation to clinical teams for high-severity events, improving response consistency.
Experiment with a rotating on-call cross-coverage system that pairs experienced staff with newer technicians to accelerate learning and maintain safety.
review: This lever governs the tension between safety coverage and personnel workload, but the options overlook transition costs and onboarding time for cross-training. Additionally, it risks uneven coverage if turnover is high.

name: Privacy-preserving video handling and consent-for-video workflow
consequences: Pulling this lever defines how video data are captured, stored, and accessed, making privacy safeguards central to research utility. Privacy-preserving configurations can reduce participant concerns and regulatory risk, but may constrain certain analytical approaches or require additional infrastructure and workflow complexity. Downstream, the need for consent administration and data access governance can affect recruitment speed and data sharing timelines.
options: Implement a consent-first, minimum-identity video capture framework with selective blurring and local pre-screening to protect privacy.
Adopt a tiered access model for video data where de-identified clips are shared for research while raw footage remains bound by strict approvals.
Design an opt-in video logging protocol that collects baseline data without recording sensitive surroundings, balancing transparency and data utility.
review: This lever governs the tension between privacy safeguards and data utility, but the options miss downstream considerations around consent administration and data access costs. Operational costs and consent management burdens are not addressed.

name: Semi-automated event triage tool integration with dual scorer feedback loop
consequences: Pulling this lever integrates a semi-automated event triage prototype with the existing scoring workflow, aiming to reduce manual review burden while preserving accuracy. It may shift annotation throughput and timing, potentially freeing scorer time but requiring robust validation and calibration to prevent drift. Downstream, improvements in efficiency could accelerate research outputs but increase dependency on algorithmic components and cross-site data alignment.
options: Integrate a semi-automated event triage prototype that flags potential parasomnia episodes and routes them to dual scorers for adjudication.
Establish a calibration study to align algorithmic rankings with human scoring across diverse participant profiles.
Pilot a feedback loop where scorer disagreements retrain the triage model to improve accuracy over time.
review: This lever balances automation and human adjudication, but the options overlook calibration drift and scorer fatigue. It also assumes consistent scorer availability, which may be challenged during extended pilot phases.

name: Pre-admission screening enrichment via structured telehealth intake
consequences: Pulling this lever shapes the pre-admission path by expanding screening and collateral history collection, potentially improving participant safety and eligibility clarity. It can reduce late-stage screening delays but may introduce new administrative overhead and access barriers for participants with limited connectivity. Downstream, this could affect enrollment pace and data quality from the outset.
options: Institute a structured telehealth intake that expands collateral history collection and risk assessment before admission.
Pilot a standardized exclusion checklist interview to reduce dropouts and improve data quality from the outset.
Incorporate home-based pre-assessments that streamline admission scheduling and minimize late-stage screening delays.
review: This lever targets pre-admission filtering, but the options ignore potential access barriers for participants with limited telehealth connectivity. It also risks delaying enrollment if telehealth adds steps.

name: Longitudinal data integrity and artifact handling strategy
consequences: Pulling this lever emphasizes data quality across multi-modal streams and longitudinal collection, improving comparability and analysis readiness. It can enhance data reliability, but may increase upfront protocol design complexity and validation overhead. Downstream, it may affect timelines for initial publications if data cleaning becomes more rigorous.
options: Adopt a standardized artifact labeling workflow and cross-site synchronization checks to preserve data quality across modalities.
Implement automated artifact detection with human-in-the-loop review to maintain accuracy without overburdening scorers.
Establish a data reconciliation cadence that resolves mismatches between sensor streams within a defined weekly window.
review: This lever focuses on data quality and interoperability, but the options overlook governance for legacy data and version control. Implementing version control and provenance tracking will be necessary to avoid drift over time.

name: External validation and replication pathways with phase-two planning
consequences: Pulling this lever institutionalizes external credibility and cross-site replication, supporting generalizability and public trust. Coordinating across centers introduces logistical complexity and potential delays in publishing, but can broaden the evidence base and accelerate progress toward scalable methods validation. Downstream, successful replication could influence funding and partnership opportunities but requires harmonized protocols and data ownership agreements.
options: Design a phased external validation plan that engages external sleep centers to replicate methods and benchmark performance.
Draft a phase-two proposal outline and metrics to secure funding for multi-site replication and intervention testing.
Create a collaborative publication strategy that coordinates shared data and methods while preserving participant confidentiality.
review: This lever strengthens external credibility, but the options overlook alignment of protocols across sites and data ownership. It also risks inconsistent data governance across sites if standards are not harmonized.

name: Staged capacity expansion tied to data quality milestones
consequences: Phased capacity expansion ties new suites to data-quality milestones and operational readiness. This prioritizes proven processes and reduces risk of unutilized space, but delays achieving full footprint and slows participant accrual.
options: Phase the addition of sleeping suites based on achieving predefined annotation throughput and acceptable inter-rater reliability across two independent scorers.
Implement a staged expansion trigger requiring successful pilot data quality milestones before leasing or renovating additional suites.
Adopt a flexible occupancy policy that proceeds with expansion only after safety drills and staffing saturation tests meet qualitative thresholds.
review: This lever balances the tension between rapid capacity growth and ensuring data-quality controls. The options miss transition costs and scheduling disruptions that could slow recruitment and data accrual.

name: Enhanced remote safety oversight and nurse presence
consequences: Adds remote monitoring dashboards and on-call safety oversight during nighttime observation. This expands safety coverage without constant on-site presence, but relies on robust communication workflows and may increase on-call workload and overhead.
options: Deploy real-time remote monitoring dashboards with standardized escalation protocols and on-call nurse response during nighttime observation.
Reassign sleep technicians to hybrid on-site and remote oversight roles to optimize coverage and reduce fatigue.
Integrate rapid-response safety drills and emergency signaling to minimize reaction times during nocturnal events.
review: This lever highlights the tension between safety coverage breadth and on-site presence. The options may miss dependencies on reliable communication infrastructure and potential increases in on-call burdens.

name: Structured event adjudication pipeline with cross-site calibration
consequences: Improves reliability and consistency of event scoring through a calibrated adjudication workflow. The trade-off is a potential reduction in throughput and a need for dedicated calibration time and training.
options: Establish a tiered adjudication workflow with cross-site calibration sessions and weekly review of discordant cases.
Introduce a learning loop where discrepant cases trigger targeted retraining for scorers and standardization audits.
Incorporate external blinded raters for select events to improve scoring reliability and reduce scorer drift.
review: Core tension lies between timely results and scoring reliability; the options may overburden staff and slow progress if calibration cycles are too frequent.

name: Naturalistic environment optimization balancing safety and ecological validity
consequences: Enhances ecological validity and participant comfort by preserving a domestic feel while enabling rapid isolation when needed. The trade-off is increased management overhead and potential data variability across environments.
options: Redesign living spaces with modular safety features that preserve domestic ambience while enabling rapid isolation in emergencies.
Tune ambient conditions, lighting, and noise to reduce participant stress and improve data quality without compromising comfort.
Adopt flexible protocol nights that adjust monitoring intensity based on real-time safety feedback and participant tolerance.
review: The tension is between ecological realism and uniform safety controls; the options risk introducing night-to-night variability that could complicate analyses.

name: Data handling and privacy framework with tiered access control
consequences: Establishes privacy-first governance with de-identification, access-tiering, and audit trails before any external sharing. This strengthens compliance and trust but may constrain granularity and slow external collaboration.
options: Institute a privacy-first data governance plan with de-identification, access-tiering, and audit trails prior to any external sharing.
Develop a local data enclave with controlled transfer to university storage and strict access authentication.
Publish de-identified, aggregated datasets with licensing clarity to enable external validation while preserving participant privacy.
review: The core tension is openness versus privacy; the options may introduce bottlenecks in data retrieval for timely analyses.

name: Cross-institutional collaboration for methods validation and replication
consequences: Expands external validity and accelerates dissemination by validating methods across centers and sharing workflows. This requires alignment on ethics, governance, and harmonized protocols, which can introduce coordination overhead.
options: Form formal joint protocols with external sleep centers to replicate key findings and share validated workflows.
Coordinate multi-center pilot studies using harmonized sensor setups to benchmark event-triage tools across sites.
Establish shared repositories for annotated datasets and scoring guidelines to accelerate validation and peer review.
review: Core tension exists between centralized coordination and local autonomy; the options may face bureaucratic delays and policy discrepancies across institutions.
